A-007-001-001: A "Transformer"-type tuner uses a tapped inductor (an autotransformer), not a Pi network, so calling it a Pi-type tuner is the FALSE statement. The other three statements are all true of a transformer tuner: it accepts a 50 Ω input and can match a wide range of low-to-high output impedances. Watch the wording — the question asks which statement is false, so the correct choice is the one that misidentifies the circuit.
A-007-001-002: A "Series"-type tuner places a series reactance in the line; it is not a Pi network, so the false statement is the one calling it a Pi-type tuner. The other statements correctly describe a series tuner: 50 Ω input and the ability to match low-to-high impedances. The trap is that all options sound plausible, but only the wrong circuit name is false.
A-007-001-003: An L-network tuner is named for its L shape (a series element and a shunt element); the false statement here is the claim that the antenna output is always high impedance. An L-network can match either up or down depending on which side the shunt element is on, and it does suit a vertical ground-plane and a 50 Ω input. So the misleading absolute "high impedance" output is the incorrect description.
A-007-001-004: A Pi tuner is built from two shunt capacitors and a series inductor in a π shape, so it is not a series-type tuner — that is the false statement. The genuine facts are that it has a 50 Ω input, matches low-to-high impedances, and is correctly called a Pi-type circuit. The distractor mislabels the network as a series type.
A-007-001-005: A pi-network is named for its π shape and consists of one inductor and two capacitors (or two inductors and one capacitor), giving the series-shunt arrangement that transforms impedance. The four-reactor and "isolated from ground" answers describe other circuits, and "power incidence network" is meaningless. The defining feature is the three-element L-C topology shaped like the Greek letter π.
A-007-001-006: Of the choices, the Pi-network offers the greatest impedance transformation (matching) ratio because its two adjustable capacitors plus inductor give a wide tuning range. Chebyshev and Butterworth are filter response types, not matching topologies, and an L-network has a fixed, limited ratio set by the two impedances. So the Pi-network wins on transformation range.
A-007-001-007: An L-network has only two components, so once you pick the two impedances to match, the component values (and Q) are fixed — it can match only one specific impedance transformation, giving it a small matching range. It is not limited by thermal stability, self-resonance, or power handling. Adding a third element (as in a Pi or T) restores adjustability and range.
A-007-001-008: A matching network transforms impedance by using reactive elements to cancel the load's reactance and to scale the resistive part up or down to the desired value. It does not create negative resistance or transconductance, and it does not literally substitute its own resistance for the load. The key idea is reactance cancellation plus resistance transformation, done with lossless L and C.
A-007-001-009: A pi-L network adds a second L-section after the pi-section, and the extra low-pass stage gives greater harmonic suppression than a plain pi-network. Both can match a multiband antenna, but the pi-L's added filtering keeps harmonics out of the antenna better. So the advantage is harmonic suppression, not efficiency or transformation range.
A-007-001-010: The Pi-L network provides the greatest harmonic suppression because it is effectively a pi-section followed by an additional L-section, adding an extra low-pass pole. A plain pi-network and an L-network roll off harmonics less steeply, and an "inverse pi-network" is not the standard high-suppression choice. More cascaded low-pass stages mean steeper attenuation of harmonics.
A-007-001-011: A Smith Chart is a graphical tool that lets you solve transmission-line and impedance-matching problems by reading points and moving along circles, simplifying otherwise messy complex-number math. It is not limited to those problems only, and it is not for DC circuits. Its real value is replacing calculation with a visual construction on the complex reflection-coefficient plane.
A-007-002-001: A quarter-wave line acts as an impedance inverter: it transforms the far-end impedance to its inverse referred to the line's characteristic impedance (Zin = Z0² / Zload). With the far end shorted (Zload = 0), the source sees a very high impedance. This is why a shorted quarter-wave stub behaves like a parallel-resonant (open) circuit at that frequency.
A-007-002-002: Because a quarter-wave line inverts impedance (Zin = Z0² / Zload), an open far end (Zload = infinity) is transformed into a very low impedance at the source. So an open quarter-wave stub looks like a short circuit. This is the mirror image of the shorted quarter-wave case, which looks open.
A-007-002-003: A half-wave line repeats the far-end impedance exactly back to the source (Zin = Zload), since it is two quarter-wave inversions in series that cancel. An open far end therefore presents a very high impedance at the source. The half-wave "repeater" property is why an open half-wave line still looks open.
A-007-002-004: A half-wave line reflects the load impedance straight through unchanged (Zin = Zload), because two successive quarter-wave inversions cancel. With the far end shorted, the source sees a very low impedance — a short still looks like a short. This repeating behaviour is the defining trait of a half-wavelength line.
A-007-002-005: Velocity factor is the speed of the wave travelling along the line divided by the speed of light in a vacuum, always a number less than 1. It is not light speed multiplied by anything, nor a shielding or impedance ratio. It tells you how much slower a signal moves in the cable than in free space.
A-007-002-006: The ratio of the actual signal velocity in a line to the speed of light in a vacuum is called the velocity factor. Characteristic (surge) impedance describes the line's Z0, and SWR describes standing waves — neither is a velocity ratio. Velocity factor is what makes a cable's physical length shorter than its electrical length.
A-007-002-007: Coaxial cable with a solid polyethylene dielectric typically has a velocity factor of about 0.66, meaning signals travel at about 66% of light speed inside it. A value of 2.7 is impossible (it would exceed light speed), and 0.33 or 0.1 are far too low for common coax. Remember 0.66 as the standard figure for solid-PE coax.
A-007-002-008: The velocity factor is set by the dielectric (insulating) material between the conductors, because VF ≈ 1/√(dielectric constant). Line length, centre-conductor resistivity, and terminating impedance do not change how fast the wave propagates. Change the dielectric and you change the velocity factor.
A-007-002-009: RF energy travels slower than the speed of light inside coax because of the dielectric, so a given electrical length (in wavelengths) corresponds to a shorter physical length. That is exactly what the velocity factor measures. Skin effect and parallel-line impedance are unrelated to why physical length is shorter than electrical length.
A-007-002-010: Velocity factor equals 1/√(dielectric constant) — the reciprocal of the square root of the dielectric constant of the insulation between the conductors. VSWR, impedance, and losses are not given by this formula. So a higher dielectric constant lowers the velocity factor.
A-007-002-011: Velocity factor is the ratio of the propagation velocity in the transmission line to the propagation velocity in free space, always less than 1. It is not the line's impedance, nor the free-space speed itself, nor anything about standing-wave reflection. It quantifies how much the line slows the wave compared with free space.
A-007-003-001: Feeding the driven element in two places, symmetrically spaced a fraction of a wavelength on each side of centre, is the T match — its balanced, two-sided connection steps the antenna's low impedance up to the line. The gamma match is the unbalanced (one-sided) version of this idea, and a stub match uses a shorted line section instead. The two symmetric tap points are the giveaway for a T match.
A-007-003-002: Feeding the driven element at the centre and at one point off to a single side is the unbalanced gamma match — "unbalanced" and "one side" point to gamma. The T match is the balanced two-sided version, and the stub match uses a separate line section. The single off-centre tap with a series capacitor is the hallmark of the gamma match.
A-007-003-003: Connecting a short section of transmission line (a stub) near the antenna, perpendicular to the feedline, is the stub match; its length and position are tuned to cancel reactance and match impedance. Gamma and delta matches connect directly to the driven element instead. The separate shorted/open line section is what defines a stub match.
A-007-003-004: Physical length = (free-space quarter wavelength) × velocity factor, where free-space λ/4 = 75 / f(MHz) metres. At 14.1 MHz, λ/4 = 75 / 14.1 = 5.32 m, then × 0.66 = 3.51 m (11.5 ft). The velocity factor shortens the cable, so always multiply the free-space length by VF.
A-007-003-005: An unbalanced feed where the coax braid goes to the element centre and the centre conductor connects through a series variable capacitor to an adjustable rod on one side of the element is the gamma match. "Lambda," "zeta," and "T" do not describe this single-sided arrangement (the T match is two-sided and balanced). The series capacitor plus one-sided rod identifies it as gamma.
A-007-003-006: Physical length = (free-space quarter wavelength) × velocity factor, with free-space λ/4 = 75 / f(MHz) metres. At 15 MHz, λ/4 = 75 / 15 = 5 m, then × 0.8 = 4 m (13.1 ft). The 0.8 velocity factor shrinks the 5 m free-space length to 4 m.
A-007-003-007: Matching a driven element with a single adjustable rod and a series capacitor (one mechanical and one capacitive element on one side) describes the gamma match. The T match uses two symmetric rods, the omega match adds a second capacitor, and a "Y" match is not a standard term. One rod plus one capacitor equals gamma.
A-007-003-008: In a gamma match the coaxial braid (shield) connects to the centre of the driven element, which is the electrical low-impedance point. The centre conductor goes through the series capacitor to the gamma rod instead. So braid-to-element-centre is the correct connection.
A-007-003-009: The centre of the driven element in a gamma match connects to the coaxial braid (shield), grounding the element centre. The coax centre conductor connects through the variable capacitor to the gamma rod, not directly to the element centre. Element centre to braid is the rule.
A-007-003-010: The adjustable gamma rod connects to the variable (series) capacitor, which in turn ties to the coax centre conductor; sliding the rod tap and tuning the capacitor sets the match. The rod does not connect to the reflector or directly to the element centre. Rod-to-capacitor is the correct link.
A-007-003-011: In a gamma match the variable capacitor connects to the adjustable gamma rod (and on its other end to the coax centre conductor), forming the series-capacitor/rod tuning path. It does not connect to a director, the element centre, or the braid. Capacitor-to-rod completes the matching network.
A-007-004-001: On a half-wave dipole the standing-wave pattern puts the voltage maximum at each open end, because the open ends cannot carry current and the wave reflects there as a voltage antinode. Current, not voltage, is what is lowest at the ends. High voltage at the tips is why dipole ends can arc at high power.
A-007-004-002: Current is lowest (a node, near zero) at the open ends of a half-wave dipole, since current cannot flow past the open tips. Voltage is the quantity that is highest there. The current node at each end pairs with a voltage antinode.
A-007-004-003: A centre-fed half-wave antenna is fed at the current maximum, which occurs at the centre, giving a convenient low feed-point impedance (~73 Ω). It is not a minimum-current or maximum-voltage point — those are at the ends. Feeding at the current maximum is why centre feeding is standard.
A-007-004-004: Voltage is lowest at the middle of a half-wave dipole, where the standing-wave pattern has a voltage node and a current antinode. Current is at its maximum there, not its minimum. Low voltage plus high current at the centre gives the low feed-point impedance.
A-007-004-005: Current is highest at the middle of a half-wave dipole, a current antinode, while voltage is at a node there. This high-current, low-voltage centre is exactly why the antenna is fed at the middle. The ends, by contrast, have high voltage and low current.
A-007-004-006: A half-wave dipole is normally fed at its centre, the point of maximum current and minimum voltage, which gives a low and convenient feed impedance (~73 Ω). It is not fed where voltage or resistance is maximum. "The antenna is resonant" is true overall but does not identify the feed point — maximum current does.
A-007-004-007: At the open ends of a half-wave dipole the voltage is high (antinode) and the current is low (node), the opposite of the centre. Both-high or both-low answers contradict the standing-wave pattern. High voltage at the tips is why end insulators and clearance matter.
A-007-004-008: Impedance equals voltage divided by current, so the centre of a half-wave antenna has low impedance because voltage is low and current is high there. Both-high or both-low conditions would not give the characteristic ~73 Ω centre value. Low V over high I equals low Z.
A-007-004-009: Minimum voltage on a half-wave dipole occurs at the centre, where the standing-wave pattern has a voltage node. The ends have maximum voltage, not minimum. The centre voltage minimum coincides with the current maximum.
A-007-004-010: Minimum current on a half-wave dipole occurs at both ends, where the open tips force current nodes. The centre has maximum current, not minimum. Current minima at the ends pair with voltage maxima.
A-007-004-011: Minimum impedance occurs at the centre of a half-wave dipole because impedance is voltage divided by current, and the centre has low voltage with high current. The ends, with high voltage and low current, have very high impedance. So minimum Z is at the centre.
A-007-005-001: Circularly polarized waves are those whose electric field vector rotates steadily as the wave travels, tracing a helix rather than staying in one plane. It is not the field physically bent into a circle, nor waves circling the earth, nor simply any loop-antenna output. The defining feature is the continuously rotating electric field.
A-007-005-002: Two crossed dipoles fed 90 degrees out of phase produce circular polarization, because the two perpendicular fields combine with a quarter-cycle time offset to make the resultant field rotate. They do not cancel; the phase offset is exactly what creates rotation. This is a common way to build a circularly polarized antenna for satellite work.
A-007-005-003: A loaded helical-wound antenna is a physically short, inductively loaded vertical (a "rubber duck" style) and radiates linear polarization, so it does NOT produce circular polarization. Crossed dipoles fed 90 degrees apart, the Lindenblad, and the axial-mode helical all do produce circular polarization. Do not confuse the small loaded helix with the large axial-mode helical beam.
A-007-005-004: Doppler shift matters most for contacts via satellite, because the satellite moves rapidly relative to the ground station, shifting the received frequency noticeably at VHF/UHF. Hilltop repeaters, handheld simplex, and slow terrestrial mobiles produce negligible Doppler. The high relative velocity of a satellite is the cause.
A-007-005-005: Crossing two linearly polarized antennas at 90 degrees causes a large polarization mismatch, giving a theoretical loss of 20 dB or more over a fixed path. The smaller 3, 6, or 10 dB figures understate it; in theory the loss approaches infinity but practically 20+ dB is observed. Full cross-polarization is a severe mismatch.
A-007-005-006: Newtonian is a reflecting-telescope configuration, not a parabolic-dish feed arrangement, so it is NOT a valid dish illumination method. Front feed, offset feed, and Cassegrain are all real ways to illuminate a parabolic reflector. The trap is borrowing the optical telescope term where it does not apply to RF dish feeds.
A-007-005-007: A parabola is efficient because its geometry focuses all the parallel incoming energy onto a single focal point where the pickup (feed) antenna sits, concentrating the captured signal. It is not about needing no matching or using a particular dipole or horn shape. The focusing of energy to the focal point is the key.
A-007-005-008: A right-hand circularly polarized helical-beam antenna best receives right-hand polarized signals; matching the sense of rotation gives maximum response. A left-hand signal would be strongly rejected (large mismatch loss). Polarization sense must match for circular antennas.
A-007-005-009: A quad antenna responds to both vertical and horizontal polarization because its loop has conductor segments oriented in both directions, picking up either field component. A helical beam is circularly polarized, a folded dipole is linear, and a ground plane is vertical. The loop geometry of the quad gives the dual-polarization response.
A-007-005-010: For amateur parabolic reflectors the surface accuracy should be kept within about 0.1 wavelength (lambda) to avoid scattering and gain loss; errors are measured relative to wavelength, not as a fixed millimetre figure or a percentage of diameter. The 0.25 lambda figure is too loose. Tying the tolerance to wavelength is the correct approach.
A-007-005-011: A parabolic dish's gain depends on its diameter measured in wavelengths — the larger the aperture relative to wavelength, the higher the gain. Feed polarization, focal length, and dish material do not set the gain (focal length affects feed placement, not gain). Gain scales with (diameter / wavelength) squared.
A-007-006-001: Work in decibels: net gain = antenna gain minus losses = 6 dBd − 3 dB = +3 dB. Since +3 dB means doubling power, ERP = 100 W × 2 = 200 watts. Convert dB to a power ratio (3 dB = ×2) and apply it to the transmitter output.
A-007-006-002: Higher SWR means standing waves of higher current and voltage exist along the line, and the increased current and voltage drive greater I²R conductor heating and dielectric heating losses. The added loss is heat dissipated in the cable, not radiation or leakage to ground. So the cause is dielectric and conductor heat losses.
A-007-006-003: First subtract the line loss in watts: 200 W − 5 W = 195 W reaching the antenna. The antenna gain of 3 dBd doubles power (3 dB = ×2), so ERP = 195 × 2 = 390 watts. Here the loss is given in watts, so subtract it directly before applying the gain.
A-007-006-004: Effective Radiated Power is the transmitter output power minus the feedline (and connector) losses, plus the antenna gain expressed relative to a dipole (dBd). It is not pre-modulation power, raw line power, or a simple input/output ratio. ERP = power delivered to the antenna times the antenna's gain over a dipole.
A-007-006-005: Combine in decibels: net = 9 dBd − 3 dB = +6 dB. Each 3 dB doubles power, so +6 dB is ×4; ERP = 200 W × 4 = 800 watts. Add gains and subtract losses in dB first, then convert the net dB to a power ratio.
A-007-006-006: Add up the decibels: −1.30 − 0.2 + 4.50 = +3.0 dB net. A net of +3 dB doubles the power, so ERP = 100 W × 2 = 200 watts. Sum all losses and gains in dB before converting to a power multiplier.
A-007-006-007: Increasing overall station gain by 3 dB doubles the effective radiated power, because +3 dB always corresponds to a power ratio of 2. ERP does not stay the same or fall — it rises. Remember the rule: every 3 dB doubles power.
A-007-006-008: Sum the decibels: −0.8 − 0.2 + 10 = +9.0 dB net. A power ratio of +9 dB is about ×7.94 (9 dB = three 3-dB doublings, 2×2×2 = 8), so ERP ≈ 125 W × 7.94 ≈ 992 W ≈ 1000 watts. Combine all dB first, then apply the ratio to the output power.
A-007-006-009: Replacing a 3 dBd antenna with a 9 dBd antenna is a net gain change of +6 dB. Since +6 dB is two doublings (×2 then ×2 = ×4), the ERP increases by a factor of 4. Convert the dB difference, not the dB numbers themselves, into the power ratio.
A-007-006-010: Net change = 10 dBd − 1 dB = +9 dB, which is a power ratio of about ×7.94 (≈ 8). ERP ≈ 2000 W × 7.94 ≈ 15 880 W ≈ 16 000 watts PEP. Add gains and subtract losses in dB, then convert the +9 dB to roughly an eightfold increase.
A-007-006-011: Net change = 10 dBd − 1 dB = +9 dB, a power ratio of about ×7.94 (≈ 8). ERP ≈ 1000 W × 7.94 ≈ 7940 W ≈ 8000 watts PEP. As with the 2000 W case, +9 dB means roughly eight times the power.
A-007-007-001: For a horizontal Yagi over flat ground, raising the antenna lowers the main-lobe takeoff angle, because ground reflection interference moves the first lobe nearer the horizon as height increases. So the takeoff angle decreases with increasing height. Lower takeoff angles favour long-distance (DX) propagation.
A-007-007-002: A simple horizontal antenna needs to be about a half wavelength or more above ground before ground reflections shape it into a directional pattern; below that it tends toward a high-angle, nearly omnidirectional cloud-warmer. Heights of an eighth, quarter, or three-eighths wavelength are too low for significant directivity. Half a wavelength is the practical threshold.
A-007-007-003: The effective ground plane — the level from which reflections appear to occur — is not the visible surface but lies several centimetres up to about 2 metres below ground, depending on soil conductivity. Poorer soil pushes the reflection plane deeper. It is not at exact ground level or above ground.
A-007-007-004: A ground-mounted quarter-wave vertical radiates at a lower vertical angle than a dipole only a quarter wavelength high, and the lower takeoff angle is what aids long-distance contacts. Lower radiation resistance, omnidirectionality, and vertical polarization are real traits but are not the reason for better DX. The low radiation angle is the key advantage.
A-007-007-005: At exactly one-half wavelength high, ground reflection from a horizontal dipole interferes such that straight-up (vertical) radiation is effectively cancelled, concentrating energy at lower angles. The pattern is altered, not unaffected, but it does not simply produce lobes at 15 and 50 degrees or cancel side lobes. The notable effect is suppression of the overhead component.
A-007-007-006: Antenna height changes the elevation pattern, but for the horizontal (azimuthal) pattern, when a horizontal dipole is less than half a wavelength high, ground reflections significantly distort it. At greater heights the azimuthal pattern stabilizes near the free-space figure-eight. Low height plus strong ground reflection equals a distorted horizontal pattern.
A-007-007-007: For long-distance (DX) propagation the vertical radiation (takeoff) angle should be low, less than 30 degrees, so signals leave near the horizon and travel far per ionospheric hop. High angles (45–90 degrees) send energy nearly straight up, good only for short range or NVIS. Lower angle means longer reach.
A-007-007-008: Decreasing the antenna's vertical radiation angle lets each ionospheric hop cover more ground, so multiple-hop paths span greater total distance. Power, antenna height (directly), and element length are not what extends multi-hop range — the takeoff angle is. A lower angle stretches each hop.
A-007-007-009: A dipole mounted very high (more than about 3 wavelengths up) approaches its free-space centre impedance of roughly 73 Ω, nearest the 75 Ω choice. Near ground the impedance varies widely, but at great height ground effects fade out. So 75 Ω is the expected value.
A-007-007-010: A horizontal antenna mounted low radiates mostly at high angles, and the ground acts as a reflector to send energy nearly straight up and back down nearby — ideal for short-range (NVIS-style) contacts on lower HF bands. It is not about noise temperature, low angle, or higher radiation resistance. The low height plus ground reflection gives high-angle coverage for close-in work.
A-007-007-011: NVIS works by firing nearly straight up so signals reflect back down over a wide local area, which calls for a horizontal antenna less than 1/4 wavelength above ground and a frequency below the current critical frequency (so the ionosphere still reflects the high-angle signal). A half-wave-high antenna radiates too low for NVIS, and verticals radiate at low angles. Low antenna plus below-critical frequency is the recipe.
A-007-008-001: Radiation resistance is the equivalent resistance that would dissipate the same power that the antenna actually radiates into space — a useful fiction for accounting for radiated power as if it were resistive loss. It is not an atmospheric resistance, the antenna's specific impedance, or combined ohmic/line losses. It represents radiated power as an equivalent resistance.
A-007-008-002: Knowing the radiation resistance lets you match the feedline and transmitter to the antenna for maximum power transfer, since it is the resistive part of the feed-point impedance. It is not used to measure near-field density or compute front-to-side or front-to-back ratios. Impedance matching is the practical reason.
A-007-008-003: Radiation resistance depends on the antenna's surroundings (nearby objects, height over ground) and on the conductor's length-to-diameter ratio, which together set how effectively it radiates. It is not fixed for all antennas, nor set by feedline length or sunspots. Geometry and environment determine it.
A-007-008-004: The ratio of radiation resistance to total resistance is the antenna efficiency, since it tells what fraction of input power is radiated rather than lost as heat. Beamwidth, ERP, and radiation conversion loss are different quantities. Efficiency = radiated portion / total.
A-007-008-005: Total antenna-system resistance is the radiation resistance plus the ohmic (loss) resistance of conductors, ground, and connections. It does not include transmission-line resistance or any "space impedance" term in this definition. Radiation resistance accounts for useful power, ohmic resistance for wasted heat.
A-007-008-006: Beamwidth is found by locating the two angles on either side of the main lobe where the signal has dropped 3 dB (to half power) from the peak, then taking the angular difference between them. It is not measured from element geometry or from front-to-side/front-to-back ratios. The 3 dB (half-power) points define beamwidth.
A-007-008-007: Percent efficiency = (radiation resistance / total resistance) × 100, the fraction of power radiated rather than lost, expressed as a percent. Dividing total by radiation resistance, or using transmission resistance, gives the wrong formula. Radiation over total, times 100.
A-007-008-008: Radiation resistance is the equivalent resistance that would dissipate the same energy the antenna radiates into space. The j factor and K factor are unrelated, and "antenna resistance" is a vaguer term that can include losses. The specific definition of equal-energy-equivalent resistance is radiation resistance.
A-007-008-009: Antenna beamwidth is the angular distance between the two half-power points (where power is down 3 dB) on the major lobe. It is measured on the main lobe, not on a minor lobe, and uses the 3 dB (half-power), not 6 dB, points. Half-power points on the major lobe define beamwidth.
A-007-008-010: Efficiency = radiation resistance / total resistance × 100, where total = radiation + ohmic = 72 + 2 = 74 Ω. So efficiency = 72 / 74 × 100 = 97.3%. The small 2 Ω of loss against 72 Ω of radiation gives a very efficient antenna.
A-007-008-011: Efficiency = radiation resistance / (radiation + ohmic) × 100 = 50 / (50 + 2) × 100 = 50 / 52 × 100 = 96.15%. Both resistances are in milliohms, but the units cancel in the ratio so you can use the numbers directly. Even tiny loss against the radiation resistance keeps efficiency high.
A-007-009-001: Waveguide is practical only at very high (microwave) frequencies, typically above 3000 MHz, because its physical size must be comparable to the wavelength — at low frequencies it would be impossibly large. The below-150 MHz and below-1500 MHz options are wrong for this reason. Above 3000 MHz the wavelength is small enough for reasonable waveguide dimensions.
A-007-009-002: Waveguide is efficient because it has low radiation, dielectric (it is air-filled), and copper losses — but "low hysteresis loss" is not a meaningful reason, since hysteresis applies to magnetic materials, not a hollow conductor. The question asks which is NOT correct, so the hysteresis answer is the odd one out. Waveguide's low loss comes from its air dielectric and large conductive surfaces.
A-007-009-003: The major advantage of waveguide is its very low loss at microwave frequencies compared with coax. Being expensive, heavy, and hard to install are disadvantages, and "frequency sensitive based on dimensions" describes a limitation, not an advantage. Low loss is the benefit.
A-007-009-004: A rectangular waveguide will not propagate energy unless its wide dimension is at least one-half wavelength, which sets its cutoff frequency. Smaller than half a wavelength and the wave is cut off and cannot pass. The half-wavelength minimum is the key dimensional rule.
A-007-009-005: Waveguide behaves like a high-pass filter: it has low loss above its cutoff frequency and very high loss (no propagation) below cutoff — so the statement claiming the opposite (low loss below cutoff) is NOT correct. The TE and TM mode descriptions are accurate. Remember: waveguide passes frequencies above cutoff, blocks those below.
A-007-009-006: Waveguide's major advantage over coax at microwave frequencies is its very low loss. The 1.8 MHz–24 GHz range is wrong (waveguide cannot work at HF), and waveguide is neither easy nor cheap to install. Low loss is the standout benefit at microwaves.
A-007-009-007: A transmission line formed as a conductor trace over a ground plane on a printed circuit board is called microstripline (microstrip). "Dielectric substrate" and "ground plane" are parts of it, not the line's name, and "dielectric imprinting" is not a term. Microstripline is the PCB transmission line.
A-007-009-008: Because microstrip's conductor is exposed on the board surface (only the ground plane is beneath it), it has poorer shielding than coaxial cable, which fully surrounds the centre conductor. It need not have especially low or high characteristic impedance. The open structure means more radiation and pickup than coax.
A-007-009-009: A section of waveguide acts like a high-pass filter: signals above the cutoff frequency pass with low loss while those below cutoff are blocked. It does not behave like a low-pass or band-stop filter. The cutoff behaviour gives it its high-pass character.
A-007-009-010: Stripline is a printed-circuit transmission line, with a conductor sandwiched between two ground planes inside the board dielectric. It is not a semiconductor family, an antenna, or a cleaning fluid. Like microstrip, it is a PCB transmission-line technique used at high frequencies.
A-007-009-011: Before working on a microwave feed horn or waveguide, make sure the transmitter is off and its power source is disconnected, because microwave RF energy can cause serious internal heating and burns to body tissue. Weather, propagation conditions, and clothing are not the safety priority here. Removing the RF hazard by powering down and disconnecting is the essential precaution.
